# We should invest in onboarding, not retention experiments

Over the last quarter the growth team has run six retention experiments
aimed at the day-thirty cohort: a re-engagement email sequence, two
in-product nudge variants, a personalised digest, a referral-credit
incentive, and a streak mechanic. The results have been, in aggregate,
unimpressive. The best of them moved day-thirty retention by a fraction of
a percentage point, well within the noise band of our weekly cohort
variance. The streak mechanic moved it backwards by a measurable amount.

Meanwhile, the activation funnel — the steps a new user goes through in
their first session — has not been touched in nearly a year. Our internal
analytics show that of users who complete all four activation steps in
their first session, sixty-eight per cent are still active on day thirty.
Of users who complete three of the four, that figure drops to forty-one
per cent. Of users who complete two or fewer, it is eleven per cent. The
gradient is steep and it is consistent across acquisition channel,
geography, and device class.

The inference I want the team to draw from these two facts is direct. The
retention experiments have failed to move the needle because they are
operating on the wrong cohort: by day thirty, the user's activation state
is already locked in, and a streak mechanic cannot rescue a user who never
saw the value of the product in the first place. We are spending engineering
capacity on the symptom and not the cause.

The proposal, then, is to redirect the growth team's next two sprints away
from retention experiments and onto the activation funnel itself. Concrete
candidates include reordering step three (which has the highest drop-off),
testing a guided-tour variant against the current self-serve flow, and
shipping a single-action shortcut for users arriving from our highest-intent
acquisition channels. The activation gradient tells us exactly where the
leverage is. We should go and pull on it.
